perturbation.original_text: 6.3 Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and ---------------------- shall inure to the benefit of and be enforceable by the parties and their respective successors and assigns, including without limitation by merger or otherwise. This Agreement shall not be assignable.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and ---------------------- shall inure to the benefit of and be enforceable by the parties and their respective successors and assigns, including without limitation by merger or otherwise. This Agreement shall not be assignable.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Successors and Assigns' clause into Article III (Agreement Not to Compete) makes it seem as if the clause only applies to that specific article, implying that successors and assigns are only bound by the non-compete provisions and not the entire agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law states that when there is a merger or consolidation, all debts, liabilities and duties of the merging/consolidated corporation automatically attach to the surviving corporation, and may be enforced against it to the same extent as if said debts, liabilities and duties had been incurred or contracted by it. By moving this to the non-compete section, it could be misinterpreted that the liabilities do not carry over.

General Corporation Law Subchapter IX. Merger, Consolidation or Conversion Â§ 251. Merger or consolidation of domestic corporations [For application of this section, see 79 Del. Laws, c. 327, Â§ 8; 80 Del. Laws, c. 265, Â§ 17; and 82 Del. Laws, c. 256, Â§ 24]. (a) Any 2 or more corporations of this State may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations or may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) The board of directors of each corporation which desires to merge or consolidate shall adopt a resolution approving an agreement of merger or consolidation and declaring its advisability. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation, that the certificate of incorporation of the resulting corporation shall be as is set forth in an attachment to the agreement; (5) The manner, if any, of converting the shares of each of the constituent corporations into shares or other securities of the corporation surviving or resulting from the merger or consolidation, or of cancelling some or all of such shares, and, if any shares of any of the constituent corporations are not to remain outstanding, t
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by relocating the 'Successors and Assigns' clause. This relocation implies that the clause only applies to Article III (Agreement Not to Compete). According to the Law Explanation, Delaware law ensures that, in cases of mergers or consolidations, the surviving corporation assumes all debts, liabilities, and duties of the merged entity. By confining the 'Successors and Assigns' clause to the non-compete section, the modified text weakens the agreement's overall enforceability concerning liabilities beyond the non-compete provisions. The scraped snippet 1 from the Delaware Code Title 8, Chapter 1, particularly emphasizes the comprehensive nature of merger and consolidation, implying that all liabilities should transfer. The relocation of the clause creates a misleading structural flaw, making it difficult to interpret if successors are bound by the entire agreement or only the non-compete, thus contradicting the intended comprehensive transfer of liabilities according to Delaware law.
